elements.sent-2411-001: Adapting pre-trained deep learning models to customized tasks has become a popular choice for developers to cope with limited computational resources and data volume.
elements.sent-2411-002: More specifically, probing--training a downstream head on a pre-trained encoder--has been widely adopted in transfer learning, which helps to prevent overfitting and catastrophic forgetting.
elements.sent-2411-003: However, such generalizability of pre-trained encoders raises concerns about the potential misuse of probing for harmful intentions, such as discriminatory speculation and warfare applications.
elements.sent-2411-004: In this work, we introduce EncoderLock, a novel applicability authorization method designed to protect pre-trained encoders from malicious probing, i.e., yielding poor performance on specified prohibited domains while maintaining their utility in authorized ones.
elements.sent-2411-005: Achieving this balance is challenging because of the opposite optimization objectives and the variety of downstream heads that adversaries can utilize adaptively.
elements.sent-2411-006: To address these challenges, EncoderLock employs two techniques: domain-aware weight selection and updating to restrict applications on prohibited domains/tasks, and self-challenging training scheme that iteratively strengthens resistance against any potential downstream classifiers that adversaries may apply.
elements.sent-2411-007: Moreover, recognizing the potential lack of data from prohibited domains in practical scenarios, we introduce three EncoderLock variants with different levels of data accessibility: supervised (prohibited domain data with labels), unsupervised (prohibited domain data without labels), and zero-shot (no data or labels available).
elements.sent-2411-008: Extensive experiments across fifteen domains and three model architectures demonstrate EncoderLock's effectiveness over baseline methods using non-transferable learning.
elements.sent-2411-009: Additionally, we verify EncoderLock's effectiveness and practicality with a real-world pre-trained Vision Transformer (ViT) encoder from Facebook.
elements.sent-2411-010: These results underscore the valuable contributions EncoderLock brings to the development of responsible AI.
elements.sent-2411-011: As the complexity of learning tasks increases, leveraging pre-trained models becomes a popular strategy for developers to train their customized models efficiently.
elements.sent-2411-012: Among various transfer learning methods, model probing has emerged as one of the most common and lightweight strategies to utilize pre-learned knowledge effectively~\cite{belinkov2022probing, cao2022putting, white2021non .}
elements.sent-2411-013: It involves freezing the encoder parts of pre-trained models while fine-tuning only the downstream heads.
elements.sent-2411-014: The encoders often include early layers of pre-trained models with more complex structures, which is responsible for extracting useful information from raw data to latent representations, on which downstream heads perform specific tasks such as classification and generation~\cite{he2022masked, le2020contrastive .}
elements.sent-2411-015: Probing offers several advantages, including resource efficiency, because of its low requirements on data and computational resources, and semantic consistency, as it helps avoid catastrophic forgetting--the performance reduction due to the encoder's loss of pre-learned knowledge after extensive fine-tuning~\cite{chen2019catastrophic, chronopoulou2019embarrassingly, iman2023review .}
elements.sent-2411-016: Furthermore, probing allows the pre-trained encoder to be used as a black-box, either as local private models~\cite{dong2023puma, gupta2023sigma, liu2021secdeep or cloud services through APIs~\cite{clarifai, openai, qu2023reaas , ensuring better intellectual property protection~\cite{tan2022federated .}}}
elements.sent-2411-017: However, the general availability of the pre-trained encoder for probing also raises concerns about malicious probing, i.e., users can probe the encoder for unethical or harmful tasks~\cite{cheatham2019confronting .}
elements.sent-2411-018: Examples include building classification heads for discriminatory speculation~\cite{internationalwomensday_gender_ai, whitehouse_ai_bill_of_rights or autonomous weapons in warfare applications~\cite{marr2018artificial .}}
elements.sent-2411-019: To address these concerns, model owners have set strict policies regarding the utilization of pre-trained encoders.
elements.sent-2411-020: For instance, OpenAI\footnote{\url{https://openai.com/policies/terms-of-use explicitly prohibits users from employing their encoder services for ``any illegal, harmful, or abusive activity".}}
elements.sent-2411-021: However, relying solely on policies, without concrete technological barriers, is insufficient to prevent model misuse.
elements.sent-2411-022: Considering malicious probing not only poses ethical risks but also represents a serious form of infringement on the intellectual property of model owners, design-time countermeasures are urgently needed for protecting the encoders with applicability authorization~\cite{wang2022non .}
elements.sent-2411-023: Proactively preventing pre-trained encoders from malicious probing presents three challenges.
elements.sent-2411-025: The protection strategy should maintain the encoder's functionality on authorized domains (those for which the encoder is designed), while restricting misuse on prohibited domains (those not allowed due to malicious intent).
elements.sent-2411-026: Furthermore, it is advisable to have a small impact on admissible domains (those are gray-listed and not explicitly considered during encoder design).
elements.sent-2411-028: Malicious users can customize downstream heads with various configurations (e.g., hyper-parameters and classifier architectures).
elements.sent-2411-029: The protection method must be robust against these diverse setups.
elements.sent-2411-031: Effective protection requires pre-defined prohibited domains, while a lack of samples from these domains can significantly impact its performance.
elements.sent-2411-032: A few studies design protection against direct applicability on prohibited tasks--malicious users can do inference but no further fine-tuning~\cite{wang2023model, wang2022non .}
elements.sent-2411-033: They introduced a training strategy considering solely a given prohibited dataset with clear labels and an authorized dataset, called {\em Non-Transferable Learning (NTL) .}
elements.sent-2411-034: Unfortunately, NTL doesn't apply to pre-trained encoders--as malicious users can further probe encoders with downstream heads using prohibited data.
elements.sent-2411-035: Therefore, we propose \MyMethod, a new applicability authorization strategy for pre-trained encoders.

Users can utilize them for various customized tasks by probing with downstream heads. \MyMethod aims to prevent malicious probing to pre-defined prohibited domains, which may have different levels of data accessibility, marked by different colors.} \label{fig: motivation-example}
elements.sent-2411-036: These levels represent real-world model providers with different data accessibility.
elements.sent-2411-037: Throughout the paper, we will use this color coding to represent these data accessibility levels.
elements.sent-2411-038: First, we propose domain-aware weight selection and updating, which identifies critical weights to the target domain and adjusts them, successfully restricting the model's transferability to the target domain while minimizing its effect on other authorized domains (addressing Challenge 1).
elements.sent-2411-039: To ensure the robustness of \MyMethod against customized malicious downstream heads (addressing Challenge 2), we introduce a minimax optimization--self-challenging training, which refines the encoder's feature space iteratively by continuously adjusting auxiliary downstream heads.
elements.sent-2411-040: Together, these strategies constitute \colorbox{koblue!20 {supervised \MyMethod , which effectively addresses the Level 1 scenario.}}
elements.sent-2411-041: To address Challenge 3, we extend two \MyMethod variants for stricter accessibility to the prohibited domain.
elements.sent-2411-042: For Level 2 where only an unlabeled target dataset is available, we introduce \colorbox{darkblue!20 {unsupervised \MyMethod , including a novel regularization term based on contrastive loss in the feature space, which deliberately obfuscates features in the target dataset.}}
elements.sent-2411-043: For Level 3, we propose \colorbox{kopink!20 {zero-shot \MyMethod , which leverages an AI agent and a text-to-image generative model to build a reliable pathway from semantic description to an unlabeled synthetic dataset.}}
elements.sent-2411-044: To ensure the synthetic dataset is representative of the target domain and comprehensive, we propose a prompt refining method utilizing the AI agent.
elements.sent-2411-045: Our Contributions: We propose \MyMethod, a novel and proactive protection on the pre-trained encoder against malicious probing.
elements.sent-2411-046: The contributions of this work include:.
elements.sent-2411-047: It maintains the encoder's performance on authorized domains with the domain-aware weight selection algorithm and offers robust defense against diverse customized probing through a self-challenging training scheme.
elements.sent-2411-048: We propose a three-level threat model following the practical data availability of representation learning.
elements.sent-2411-049: Correspondingly, we present three variants of \MyMethod with novel techniques to address different levels of target domain data accessibility, tackling realistic comprehensive scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-050: We conduct extensive experiments to evaluate \MyMethod across twelve domains and three encoder architectures, including a large, real-world Vision Transformer~\cite{caron2021emerging .}
elements.sent-2411-051: Our results demonstrate the effectiveness of all three \MyMethod variants.
elements.sent-2411-052: Specifically, we assess \MyMethod in a real applicability authorization scenario, preventing a pre-trained encoder from being misused for military purposes while keeping its generalizability to civilian ones.
elements.sent-2411-053: Pre-trained models are widely used in computer vision~\cite{marcelino2018transfer, parisi2022unsurprising, yuan2021florence , representation learning~\cite{bengio2013representation, shen2021financial, yi2022graph, zhong2016overview , and natural language processing~\cite{han2021pre, qiu2020pre, wang2022pre , which embed pre-learned knowledge as the model initialization to reduce the complexity in training new tasks.}}}

elements.sent-2411-057: Full Fine-tuning: Full fine-tuning leverages the entire pre-trained model as the training initialization and fine-tunes it with the target dataset.
elements.sent-2411-058: It often has good performance but has the risk of stability and catastrophic forgetting~\cite{chen2019catastrophic, tajbakhsh2016convolutional .}
elements.sent-2411-059: Prompting: Rather than finetune the model parameters, prompting redirects the pre-trained model via modification on the inputs (i.e., visual prompt).
elements.sent-2411-060: Prompting is efficient but performance experiences a larger degradation~\cite{bahng2022exploring, gan2023decorate, jia2022visual, yang2024fine .}
elements.sent-2411-061: Model Probing: Probing freezes the early layers of the pre-trained model (e.g., deep convolutional layers or self-attention layers~\cite{cordonnier2019relationship, han2022survey ) as the fixed pre-trained encoder and fine-tunes the downstream classifier.}
elements.sent-2411-062: It has a small training cost and high stability of the training process~\cite{belinkov2022probing, gao2023tuning, ravichander2020probing, wu2024structured .}
elements.sent-2411-063: Probing also supports pre-trained encoders from different training schemes, which can be categorized into three types: supervised, unsupervised, and self-supervised.
elements.sent-2411-064: For supervised learning, the model (i.e., encoder and downstream head) is trained directly using labeled training data and a loss function (e.g., cross-entropy loss)~\cite{cunningham2008supervised .}
elements.sent-2411-065: Unsupervised learning aims to learn from unlabeled data, using methods such as Gaussian Mixtures Model (GMM)~\cite{reynolds2009gaussian , Variational Autoencoder (VAE)~\cite{kingma2013auto , and Generative Adversarial Network (GAN)~\cite{goodfellow2020generative .}}}
elements.sent-2411-066: Self-supervised learning aims to train an encoder to predict one part of data given another part of the input~\cite{chen2020simple, he2020momentum, jaiswal2020survey .}
elements.sent-2411-067: It leverages the inherent data characteristics and shows increasing robustness and generalizability of the encoder~\cite{hendrycks2019using .}
elements.sent-2411-068: Specifically, given the input (e.g., an image), one will use data augmentation operations (e.g., cropping, color jitter, and adding random noise) to build augmented images.
elements.sent-2411-069: The training objective is to make the encoder generate similar embeddings for augmented images from the same input, denoted as positive pairs; while ensuring the discrepancy of embeddings from different images, denoted as negative pairs.
elements.sent-2411-070: The training of a self-supervised encoder utilizes contrastive loss~\cite{chen2020simple, he2020momentum , which increases the similarity between positive pairs but decreases those of negative pairs.}
elements.sent-2411-071: Our design of \MyMethod considers various data accessibility of prohibited domains, which aligns with the training process of pre-trained encoders--with or without labeled datasets.
elements.sent-2411-072: Recently, applicability authorization, a new IP protection scheme, has been proposed to address the rising concerns of IP infringement on DNN models~\cite{wang2023model,wang2022non,zhou2024archlock, ding2025non .}
elements.sent-2411-073: Traditional model IP protection aims to protect the rights of owners of DNN models with two typical defense strategies: ownership verification and usage authorization.
elements.sent-2411-074: Ownership verification is designed to trace the illegal behavior of IP infringement using methods such as embedding watermarks during the training procedure or recording fingerprints of the model owner~\cite{jia2021entangled,yang2021robust,zhang2018protecting .}
elements.sent-2411-075: In contrast, usage authorization aims to restrict user access to the model, ensuring that only verified, trusted users can access with assigned authorization keys~\cite{alam2020deep, chakraborty2020hardware .}
elements.sent-2411-076: Instead of protecting the model parameters or hyper-parameters directly like traditional methods, applicability authorization focuses on the unauthorized transfer of the pre-trained models \cite{zhou2024archlock .}
elements.sent-2411-077: Specifically, it aims to prevent malicious transfer learning through which an attacker can abuse the pre-trained model for prohibited data or tasks, i.e., non-transfer-learning.
elements.sent-2411-078: In this work, we further propose \MyMethod to address the challenges of applicability authorization of pre-trained encoders to safeguard them from unauthorized probing.
elements.sent-2411-079: Wang et al.~\cite{wang2022non introduced NTL for applicability authorization of an entire model without any fine-tuning.}
elements.sent-2411-080: In particular, NTL leverages a negative regularization term on the model's target domain performance: { \begin{equation}L_{NTL} = L_\mathcal{S} + R_\mathcal{T} \label{eq: ntl objective}\end{equation} where $L_\mathcal{S}$ is the Kullback–Leibler (KL) divergence/loss on the source dataset, aiming to retain the model's performance on the source domain.}
elements.sent-2411-081: Model non-transferability comes from the regularization term, defined as $R_\mathcal{T =- \text{min (\beta, \alpha \cdot L_\mathcal{T \cdot L_{dis )$~\cite{wang2022non , where $L_\mathcal{T}$ is the KL loss on the target dataset, $L_{dis}$ measures the feature space distance between the source and target domains (using Maximum Mean Discrepancy), and $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are scaling factors.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-082: Another prior work~\cite{wang2023model proposes an additional CUTI-domain for regularization on private style features with the $R_\mathcal{T}$ as $-L_\mathcal{T}$.}
elements.sent-2411-083: In addition, previous works also proposed `source-only' NTL for cases when there is no target data available.
elements.sent-2411-084: As the term `source-only' indicates, this strategy leverages generative models (i.e., GAN) to create a synthetic dataset, which serves as the boundary from the source domain to prohibit the model's transferability to all other domains.
elements.sent-2411-085: Prior works focus on the case when the attacker uses the trained model directly but cannot fine-tune it~\cite{wang2023model, wang2022non .}
elements.sent-2411-086: However, with the increasing popularity and low cost of probing the pre-trained (fixed) encoder, the applicability authorization (model non-transferability) can be bypassed in a few probing epochs.

elements.sent-2411-091: Pre-trained encoders and model probing bring new challenges in data availability for applicability authorization.
elements.sent-2411-092: Our work \MyMethod aims to address them accordingly.
elements.sent-2411-093: In this work, we tackle applicability authorization for pre-trained encoders, aiming to prevent malicious users from probing the encoder for harmful tasks (i.e., unethical, illegal, or abusive activities).
elements.sent-2411-094: In this paper, we focus on vision encoders and image classification as the downstream task.

elements.sent-2411-098: Capabilities of the malicious users include:.
elements.sent-2411-099: They can probe a pre-trained encoder using inputs from prohibited target domains and utilize the representations to train a local downstream classifier for inference.
elements.sent-2411-100: Although they query the encoder (as a service or local private model), it is a black-box with both structure and parameters unknown.
elements.sent-2411-101: Users can build their own downstream classifiers, customizing the classifier's hyper-parameters and fine-tuning the parameters with any learning rates and optimizers.

elements.sent-2411-119: Preservation of Integrity: The integrity of the encoder lies in maintaining the pre-learned knowledge about the authorized domains.
elements.sent-2411-120: One question is raised: how can \MyMethod make minimal modifications to the encoder to restrict it on the prohibited domain while preserving the integrity on the source domain? Challenge 2.
elements.sent-2411-121: Robustness to malicious probing: When malicious users adjust the downstream heads for the prohibited domain with any learning rate and optimizer, how to successfully `lock' the encoder against malicious probing? Challenge 3.
elements.sent-2411-122: Different target domain data accessibility: In reality, as the defender (model owner) may have various levels of knowledge about the target domain, how should \MyMethod be designed for practical scenarios including unlabeled datasets or even no samples from prohibited domains?.

elements.sent-2411-153: With critical weights selected to update, we design a loss function in the form of Equation \eqref{eq: ntl objective , focusing on the regularization term $R_\mathcal{T}$ to mitigate the malicious probing for the target domain.}
elements.sent-2411-154: Previous regularization terms \cite{wang2023model, wang2022non only consider the target domain, which leads to unstable performance especially when $L_\mathcal{S}$ and $L_\mathcal{T}$ are at different orders of magnitude.}

elements.sent-2411-156: Therefore, we propose a new log-ratio regularization term considering both $L_\mathcal{S}$ and $L_\mathcal{T}$: { \begin{equation}L_{el}=L_\mathcal{S} + R_\mathcal{T}, \: \textnormal{where} \: R_\mathcal{T}=\log(1 + \alpha \frac{L_{\mathcal{S}}}{L_{\mathcal{T}}}) \label{eq: regularization}\end{equation} Such logarithmic regularization term gently penalizes the loss ratio between the source and target, with \( \alpha \) moderating the balance between preserving the source domain accuracy and enforcing the target domain non-transferability.}

elements.sent-2411-162: To improve robustness against any potential malicious probing for supervised \MyMethod, we propose a self-challenging training scheme with a minimax problem formulation as: { \begin{equation}\phi^*= \arg\min_{\phi}\max_{\theta_\mathcal{T}} L_{el}(\phi,\theta_\mathcal{S},\theta_\mathcal{T}) \quad \text { s.t. } \enspace \|\phi^*-\phi\|_0 \le M \label{eq: min max loss }\end{equation} Specifically, during the iterations of updating critical weights in the encoder part, we also adjust the target downstream models iteratively.}

elements.sent-2411-163: It is noted that the training objective of the target downstream models will be adversarial to \MyMethod's applicability objective---the fine-tuning aims to extract useful features in the embeddings to enhance the target domain performance.

elements.sent-2411-166: To ensure the randomness of the target downstream classifiers, every iteration we retrain it from scratch (with a random initialization).
elements.sent-2411-167: The iterative training proceeds until the target downstream classifier's accuracy drops below a predefined threshold or reaches the maximum number of altered weights $M$.
elements.sent-2411-168: The self-challenging training scheme ensures a gradual and smooth reduction in the encoder's transferability, forcing the encoder part to extract features that are less useful for the target domain, thereby leading to more robust performance even when the attacker probes the downstream model adaptively.
elements.sent-2411-169: In this section, we address Level-2 accessibility of the target domain via unsupervised \MyMethod.
elements.sent-2411-170: This scenario is practically relevant when the goal is to prevent transferring to arbitrary sets of images while getting their labels is either infeasible or expensive.
elements.sent-2411-171: Our method leverages the technique from self-supervised representation learning~\cite{chen2020simple, he2020momentum , which builds a highly distinguishable feature space without labeling.}
elements.sent-2411-172: The design idea for unsupervised \MyMethod is as follows: for the latent embeddings of samples from the source domain, we aim to ensure their high discrepancy between classes; while for those from the prohibited target domain, our objective is to obfuscate the latent clusters boundary so that the embeddings would not contain much information about the class.
elements.sent-2411-173: As shown in Fig.~\ref{fig: unsupervised-framework , an expected encoder will automatically cluster the samples from the source domain but blur the class boundaries of the target domain.}
elements.sent-2411-174: Due to such direct manipulation towards the encoder's feature space, the unsupervised \MyMethod is always robust to different downstream heads and doesn't require further self-challenging training.
elements.sent-2411-175: Towards this goal, we introduce a self-supervised regularization term $R_\mathcal{T}$ to be used in Equation \eqref{eq: regularization .}
elements.sent-2411-176: Specifically, given a batch of samples from the target domain, we leverage data augmentation, including random crop, color jitter, or Gaussian blur~\cite{shorten2019survey, mikolajczyk2018data , to create a set of positive pairs and a set of negative pairs.}

elements.sent-2411-188: In this section, we address Level-3 accessibility of the target domain for \MyMethod, where the model owner even has no target samples.
elements.sent-2411-189: This represents the most practical and relevant scenario, as the definition of harmful content is often vague in real-world applications.
elements.sent-2411-190: For instance, in most cases, a DNN product's user guidelines regulate prohibited content using text descriptions of unethical or sensitive material.
elements.sent-2411-191: How to turn such vague scope description into representative and comprehensive target domain dataset is a challenge.
elements.sent-2411-192: We define the basic knowledge about the target domain as a theme, which can be in the form of a text description, keywords, or reference figures.
elements.sent-2411-193: Using the target theme, zero-shot \MyMethod aims to generate a synthetic dataset for applicability authorization without relying on real-world samples or labels.. \yf{this sounds reasonable.}
elements.sent-2411-194: It is important for such arguments and reasoning before the specific techniques and methods of generating synthetic datasets.
elements.sent-2411-195: We encourage a wide coverage of the synthetic dataset.

elements.sent-2411-198: Fig.~\ref{fig: zero-shot-framework presents the framework of zero-shot \MyMethod, illustrating the process of generating a synthetic dataset for ``military vehicles''.}
elements.sent-2411-199: Section~\ref{exp: zero-shot encoderlock will showcase the full results.}
elements.sent-2411-200: First, we employ a large language model (e.g., GPT-4~\cite{achiam2023gpt ) as an AI agent to generate text inputs, known as prompts, for the given theme.}
elements.sent-2411-201: These prompts are then fed into pre-trained text-to-image models (e.g., CLIP~\cite{radford2021learning and Stable Diffusion~\cite{rombach2021highresolution ) to generate the synthetic dataset.}}
elements.sent-2411-202: To ensure the synthetic dataset comprehensively covers the target domain, we introduce a prompt refining framework.
elements.sent-2411-203: Using a pre-trained vision encoder, we extract latent features from the synthetic images and compute pairwise similarity scores between the initial prompts.
elements.sent-2411-204: This similarity matrix serves as feedback to the AI agent, enabling it to analyze the scores, identify similar prompts, and refine them accordingly.
elements.sent-2411-205: For example, as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig: zero-shot-framework , Antique Cannon and Futuristic Tank exhibit high similarity due to their shared barrel feature.}
elements.sent-2411-206: Consequently, the AI agent revises these prompts to be Artillery System and Combat Vehicles.
elements.sent-2411-207: The refinement process continues until all prompt pairs demonstrate low similarity or the similarity stops decreasing.
elements.sent-2411-208: Finally, we employ the synthetic dataset generated in the last round for unsupervised \MyMethod training, resulting in an encoder with restricted transferability to the target ``theme.''.

elements.section-2411-013: Impact of the Synthetic Dataset Quality on Zero-shot \MyMethod
elements.sent-2411-411: In addition to prompt relevance, the generation quality of the synthetic dataset significantly affects the performance of zero-shot \MyMethod.
elements.sent-2411-412: We measure the quality with two metrics: the noise level of the synthetic images and the generation quality of the diffusion model.
elements.sent-2411-413: To ensure a fair comparison, we use the same set of prompts (refined prompts from the prohibited domain) for zero-shot \MyMethod and select an encoder with the same degradation level on the authorized domain as reported in Section~\ref{exp: zero-shot encoderlock (greater than $92\%$).}
elements.sent-2411-414: We report the \MyMethod performance in the format of ($acc_m^\mathcal{S , acc_m^\mathcal{T}$).}
elements.sent-2411-415: Note that the defense goal of \MyMethod is a higher $acc_m^\mathcal{S}$ and lower $acc_m^\mathcal{T}$.
elements.figure-2411-024: \includegraphics[width=0.85\linewidth]{imgs/synthetic-quality-1.pdf} \caption{Zero-shot \MyMethod performance with different noise levels} \label{fig: noise}
elements.sent-2411-416: Fig.~\ref{fig: noise shows an example image with different levels of Gaussian noise.}
elements.sent-2411-417: Introducing random noise into the images reduces \MyMethod's ability to restrict the prohibited domain.
elements.sent-2411-418: We assess the impact of different noise levels, at $\sigma=1$, $\sigma=5$, and $\sigma=10$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-419: The zero-shot \MyMethod performance degrades to ($91.90\%$, $32.02\%$), ($91.85\%$, $37.13\%$), and ($92.20\%$, $46.30\%$), where the performance on the noise-free synthetic dataset is ($92.86\%$, $23.69\%$).
elements.sent-2411-420: High level of noise significantly reduces the synthetic quality, leading to poorer performance of the zero-shot \MyMethod.
elements.figure-2411-025: \includegraphics[width=0.85\linewidth]{imgs/synthetic-quality-2.pdf} \caption{Zero-shot \MyMethod performance with different diffusion qualities} \label{fig: qualities}
elements.sent-2411-421: We regenerate the synthetic datasets with varying number of inference iterations in the stable diffusion model.
elements.sent-2411-422: The generation quality improves as we increase the iteration count from $5$, $10$, $20$, to $50$ (the value used in the original setting), as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig: qualities .}
elements.sent-2411-424: A higher-quality synthetic dataset provides clearer potential features of the prohibited domain, thus enhancing the restriction performance.
elements.sent-2411-425: However, increasing the number of inference iterations in the generator leads to higher computational costs.
elements.sent-2411-426: Running 5 inference iterations achieves a speed of $8.2$ items/s, whereas 50 inference iterations reduce the speed to $0.8$ items/s.. \update{.}
elements.figure-2411-026: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{imgs/data-volume.pdf} \caption{\MyMethod performance for various volumes of probing data.} \label{fig: data-volume}
elements.sent-2411-427: We evaluate the scenario where an attacker probes the encoder using varying amounts of probing data, ranging from $10\%$ to the entire dataset, in both supervised and unsupervised settings.
elements.sent-2411-428: The results are illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig: data-volume .}
elements.sent-2411-429: Notably, for the prohibited target domain, the accuracy remains low even when the attacker utilizes the full prohibited dataset, while the protection slightly degrades with more data.
elements.sent-2411-430: For the authorized source domain, the accuracy remains consistently high even with a small amount of probing data.
elements.sent-2411-431: These results demonstrate the robustness of both supervised and unsupervised versions of \MyMethod against malicious probing attempts.
elements.sent-2411-432: In this section, we present more comparison results with baselines similar to Table~\ref{tab: comparison with baseline methods .}
